subject: Physics
q: If $M_o$ is the mass of isotope ${ }_5^{12} B, M_P$ and $M_n$ are the masses of proton and neutron, then nuclear binding energy of isotope is :
question: If $M_o$ is the mass of isotope ${ }_5^{12} B, M_P$ and $M_n$ are the masses of proton and neutron, then nuclear binding energy of isotope is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { B.E. }=\Delta m C^2 \\ & \left(5 M_p+7 M_n-M_o\right) C^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A nucleus at rest disintegrates into two smaller nuclei with their masses in the ratio of $2: 1$. After disintegration they will move :
question: A nucleus at rest disintegrates into two smaller nuclei with their masses in the ratio of $2: 1$. After disintegration they will move :
options: in the same direction with same speed.
in opposite directions with the same speed.
in opposite directions with speed in the ratio of $2: 1$ respectively.
in opposite directions with speed in the ratio of $1: 2$ respectively.
solution:
By conservation of momentum
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{f}} \\
& \mathrm{O}=\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{u}_{1+} \mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{u}_2 \\& \frac{\mathrm{u}_1}{\mathrm{u}_2}=-\left[\frac{1}{2}\right] \text { as } \frac{\mathrm{m}_1}{\mathrm{~m}_2}=\frac{2}{1}
\end{aligned}$
move in opposite direction with speed ratio $1: 2$

subject: Physics
q: The above is a plot of binding energy per nucleon $E_b$, against the nuclear mass $M ; A, B, C, D, E, F$ correspond to different nuclei. Consider four reactions: (i) $A+B \rightarrow C+\varepsilon$ (ii) $\mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}+\varepsilon$ (iii) $\mathrm{D}+\mathrm{E} \rightarrow \mathrm{F}+\varepsilon$ and (iv) $F \rightarrow D+E+\varepsilon$ where $\varepsilon$ is the energy released? In which reactions is $\varepsilon$ positive?
question: The above is a plot of binding energy per nucleon $E_b$, against the nuclear mass $M ; A, B, C, D, E, F$ correspond to different nuclei. Consider four reactions: (i) $A+B \rightarrow C+\varepsilon$ (ii) $\mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}+\varepsilon$ (iii) $\mathrm{D}+\mathrm{E} \rightarrow \mathrm{F}+\varepsilon$ and (iv) $F \rightarrow D+E+\varepsilon$ where $\varepsilon$ is the energy released? In which reactions is $\varepsilon$ positive?
solution: Reaction is fusion and $4^{\text {th }}$ reaction is fission.

subject: Physics
q: The radius of a nucleus of mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>64</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn></math> fermi. Then the mass number of another nucleus having radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> fermi is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1000</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________.
question: The radius of a nucleus of mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>64</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn></math> fermi. Then the mass number of another nucleus having radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> fermi is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1000</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________.
solution:
Radius of a nucleus is given by, R=R0 A1/3.

Therefore, R3∝A

⇒4.843=64A

⇒64 A=(1.2)3

⇒64A=1.44×1.2

⇒A=641.44×1.2=1000x

⇒x=144×1264=27

subject: Physics
q: The nuclear activity of a radioactive element becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> of its initial value in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> years. The half-life of radioactive element is ________ years.
question: The nuclear activity of a radioactive element becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> of its initial value in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> years. The half-life of radioactive element is ________ years.
solution:
A=A0e-λt

A08=A0e-λt⇒λt=ln8

λt=3ln2

ln2λ=t3=303=10 years

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following radiations has the least wavelength?
question: Which of the following radiations has the least wavelength?
solution:
$\mathrm{R} \rightarrow$ Radio waves ; $\mathrm{M} \rightarrow$ Micro waves; $\quad \mathrm{I} \rightarrow$ Infra red rays; $\mathrm{V} \rightarrow$ Visible rays; $\mathrm{U} \rightarrow$ Ultraviolet rays; $\mathrm{X} \rightarrow \mathrm{X}$ rays; $\quad \mathrm{G} \rightarrow \gamma$ rays; $\quad \mathrm{C} \rightarrow$ Cosmic rays
$\Rightarrow \gamma$ rays has least wavelength

q: Consider the nuclear fission, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ne</mi><mn>20</mn></msup><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>He</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>12</mn></msup></math>. Given that the binding energy/nucleon of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ne</mi><mn>20</mn></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>He</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mtext> </mtext></math>and<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext> </mtext><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>12</mn></msup></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8.03</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>MeV</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>7.86</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>, respectively. Identify the correct statement:
question: Consider the nuclear fission, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ne</mi><mn>20</mn></msup><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>He</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>12</mn></msup></math>. Given that the binding energy/nucleon of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ne</mi><mn>20</mn></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>He</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mtext> </mtext></math>and<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext> </mtext><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>12</mn></msup></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8.03</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>MeV</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>7.86</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>, respectively. Identify the correct statement:
options: Energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> has to be supplied.
solution:
Q=Ef-Ei
=12×7.86+4×7.07+4×7.07-(20×8.03)
Q=-9.72 MeV

subject: Physics
q: If the binding energy of ground state electron in a hydrogen atom is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>, then, the energy required to remove the electron from the second excited state of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Li</mi><msup><mo> </mo><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> will be: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
question: If the binding energy of ground state electron in a hydrogen atom is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>, then, the energy required to remove the electron from the second excited state of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Li</mi><msup><mo> </mo><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> will be: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
Given:

Binding energy, EH=E0=13.6 eV.

Now for hydrogen like atom, we can write

ELi2+=E0Z2n2=13.6×99=13.6 eV

=136×10-1 eV

Therefore, x=136.

subject: Physics
q: In a nuclear fission process, a high mass nuclide <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>≈</mo><mn>236</mn><mo>)</mo></math> with binding energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi><mo>/</mo></math>Nucleon dissociated into two middle mass nuclides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>≈</mo><mn>118</mn><mo>)</mo></math>, having binding energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi><mo>/</mo></math>Nucleon. The energy released in the process would be _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MeV</mi></math>.
question: In a nuclear fission process, a high mass nuclide <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>≈</mo><mn>236</mn><mo>)</mo></math> with binding energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi><mo>/</mo></math>Nucleon dissociated into two middle mass nuclides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>≈</mo><mn>118</mn><mo>)</mo></math>, having binding energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi><mo>/</mo></math>Nucleon. The energy released in the process would be _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MeV</mi></math>.
solution:
The formula to calculate the energy released in the given process can be written as

Q=BEProduct -BEReactant    ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

Q=2(118)(8.6)-236(7.6) MeV=236 MeV

subject: Physics
q: The half-life period of a radio-active element $\mathrm{X}$ is same as the mean life time of another radioactive element $\mathrm{Y}$. Initially they have the same number of atoms. Then
question: The half-life period of a radio-active element $\mathrm{X}$ is same as the mean life time of another radioactive element $\mathrm{Y}$. Initially they have the same number of atoms. Then
options: $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ have same decay rate initially
$\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ decay at same rate always.
solution:
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda_{\mathrm{x}}}$
$\tau_{\text {mean }}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{y}}} ; \frac{\mathrm{dN}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\lambda \mathrm{N}$
$\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda_{\mathrm{x}}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{y}}} \Rightarrow \lambda_{\mathrm{x}}=\lambda_{\mathrm{y}}(0.6932) \Rightarrow \lambda_{\mathrm{y}}>\lambda_{\mathrm{x}}$

subject: Physics
q: A nuclear transformation is denoted by $X(n, \alpha){ }_3^7 \mathrm{Li}$. Which of the following is the nucleus of element $X$ ?
question: A nuclear transformation is denoted by $X(n, \alpha){ }_3^7 \mathrm{Li}$. Which of the following is the nucleus of element $X$ ?
solution: $\mathrm{X}+{ }_0^1 \mathrm{n} \rightarrow{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }_3^7 \mathrm{Li}$

subject: Physics
q: If $\mathrm{N}_0$ is the original mass of the substance of half- life period $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=5$ years, then the amount of substance left after 15 years is
question: If $\mathrm{N}_0$ is the original mass of the substance of half- life period $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=5$ years, then the amount of substance left after 15 years is
solution: Amount left $=N_0 / 2^n=N_0 / 8 \quad($ Here $n=15 / 5=3)$

subject: Physics
q: Using a nuclear counter the count rate of emitted particles from a radioactive source is measured. At <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> it was <math><mn>1600</mn></math> counts per second and <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math> seconds it was <math><mn>100</mn></math> counts per second. The count rate observed, as counts per second, at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn></math> seconds is close to:
question: Using a nuclear counter the count rate of emitted particles from a radioactive source is measured. At <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> it was <math><mn>1600</mn></math> counts per second and <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math> seconds it was <math><mn>100</mn></math> counts per second. The count rate observed, as counts per second, at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn></math> seconds is close to:
solution:
After 8 seconds count rate changes from A1=1600 per second to A2=100 per second.

A 2 = A 1 16 = A 1 2 4

∴8s in 4 half life

⇒1 half life =2 s

t=6s is 3 half life.

∴ Count rate after 3 second

A=A123=16008=200

subject: Physics
q: Nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is having mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn></math> and its binding energy per nucleon is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>. It splits in two fragments <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> of mass numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>105</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>115</mn></math>. The binding energy of nucleons in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> per nucleon. The energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> released per fission will be:
question: Nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is having mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn></math> and its binding energy per nucleon is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>. It splits in two fragments <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> of mass numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>105</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>115</mn></math>. The binding energy of nucleons in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> per nucleon. The energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> released per fission will be:
solution:
The total binding energy of nucleus A is,

EA=220×5.6=1232 MeV

The total binding energy of B and C combined is,

EB+EC=105×6.4+115×6.4=1408 MeV

Therefore, the energy released per fission is,

Q=EB+EC-EA=1408-1232=176 MeV

subject: Physics
q:
Energy released when two deuterons $\left({ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2\right)$ fuse to form a helium nucleus $\left({ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4\right)$ is :
(Given : Binding energy per nucleon of ${ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2=1.1 \mathrm{MeV}$ and binding energy per nucleon of ${ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4=7.0 \mathrm{MeV}$)
question:
Energy released when two deuterons $\left({ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2\right)$ fuse to form a helium nucleus $\left({ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4\right)$ is :
(Given : Binding energy per nucleon of ${ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2=1.1 \mathrm{MeV}$ and binding energy per nucleon of ${ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4=7.0 \mathrm{MeV}$)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & E_B=\mathrm{BE}_{\text {reactant }}-\mathrm{BE}_{\text {product }} \\ & =1.1 \times 2+1.1 \times 2-7 \times 4=-23.6 \mathrm{MeV} \\ & =\mathrm{Q}=23.6 \mathrm{MeV}\end{aligned}$

Mass of proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00783</mn><mo> </mo><mi>U</mi></math>, mass of neutron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00867</mn><mo> </mo><mi>U</mi></math> and mass of tin nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>sn</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>119</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>902199</mn><mo> </mo><mi>U</mi></math>. (take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi>U</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>931</mn><mo> </mo><mi>M</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>V</mi></math>)

subject: Physics
q: At a given instant, say <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> two radioactive substance <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> have equal activities. The ratio <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> of their activities after time <math><mi>t</mi></math> itself decays with time <math><mi>t</mi></math> as <math><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>t</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the half-life of <math><mi>A</mi></math> is <math><mi>l</mi><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo></math> the half-life of <math><mi>B</mi></math> is:
question: At a given instant, say <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> two radioactive substance <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> have equal activities. The ratio <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> of their activities after time <math><mi>t</mi></math> itself decays with time <math><mi>t</mi></math> as <math><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>t</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the half-life of <math><mi>A</mi></math> is <math><mi>l</mi><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo></math> the half-life of <math><mi>B</mi></math> is:
solution:
Given,
Half life of A=ln2
Initial number of nucleus is, R0A=R0B=R0

Half life is related with decay constant as, t1/2A=ln2λ
λA=1

At t=0 RA=RB
From activity relation, we have, 
R0e-λAT=R0E-λBT

at T=t, ⇒RBRA=R0e-λBtR0e-λAt

As per given question, RBRA=e-3t, On comparing with above relation, we have
⇒e-λB-λAt=e-t
⇒λB-λA=3
⇒λB=3+1=4
⇒t1/2B=λn2λB=λn24

q: You are given that  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Li</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>3</mn><mn>7</mn></mmultiscripts><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0160</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo>,</mo></math>Mass of Mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>He</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>2</mn><mn>4</mn></mmultiscripts><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0026</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math> and Mass of  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>He</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>1</mn><mn>1</mn></mmultiscripts><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0079</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Li</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>3</mn><mn>7</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is converted into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mrow></mrow><mn>2</mn><mn>4</mn></msubsup></math> He by proton capture, the energy liberated, (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kWh</mi></math> ), is : [Mass of nucleon <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>GeV</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>]
question: You are given that  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Li</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>3</mn><mn>7</mn></mmultiscripts><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0160</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo>,</mo></math>Mass of Mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>He</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>2</mn><mn>4</mn></mmultiscripts><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0026</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math> and Mass of  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>He</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>1</mn><mn>1</mn></mmultiscripts><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0079</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Li</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>3</mn><mn>7</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is converted into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mrow></mrow><mn>2</mn><mn>4</mn></msubsup></math> He by proton capture, the energy liberated, (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kWh</mi></math> ), is : [Mass of nucleon <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>GeV</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>]
solution:
E=ΔmC2

E=(1.0079+7.0160-2(4.0026)×931

=1.33×106

subject: Physics
q: There are 1010 radioactive nuclei in a given radioactive element. Its half-life time is 1 min. How many nuclei will remain after 30 s?(2=1.414)
question: There are 1010 radioactive nuclei in a given radioactive element. Its half-life time is 1 min. How many nuclei will remain after 30 s?(2=1.414)
solution:
Recall the relationship between the present amount of element and the initial amount of element in terms of the decay constant, N=N0e-λt, now the relation between decay constant and half-life, t12=0.693λ, ⇒N=N0e-0.693tt12

⇒N=1010e-0.35=7×109.

question: A radioactive sample disintegrates via two independent decay processes having half lives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>T</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow></msubsup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>T</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow></msubsup></math> respectively. solution:
λeq=λ1+λ2
1 T1/2=1 T1/2(1)+1 T1/2(2)
T1/2=T1/2(1)T1/2(2)T1/2(1)+T1/2(2)

subject: Physics
q: The energy released when $\dfrac{7}{17.13}$ kg of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$ is converted into $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ by proton bombardment is $\alpha \times 10^{32}$ eV. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Nearest integer) (Mass of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li} = 7.0183$ u, mass of $^{4}_{2}\text{He} = 4.004$ u, mass of proton $= 1.008$ u and $1$ u $= 931$ MeV/c$^2$ and Avogadro number $= 6.0 \times 10^{23}$)
question: The energy released when $\dfrac{7}{17.13}$ kg of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$ is converted into $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ by proton bombardment is $\alpha \times 10^{32}$ eV. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Nearest integer) (Mass of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li} = 7.0183$ u, mass of $^{4}_{2}\text{He} = 4.004$ u, mass of proton $= 1.008$ u and $1$ u $= 931$ MeV/c$^2$ and Avogadro number $= 6.0 \times 10^{23}$)
solution:
The nuclear reaction for the proton bombardment of Lithium is:

$^{7}_{3}\text{Li} + ^{1}_{1}\text{H} \rightarrow 2 ^{4}_{2}\text{He}$

The mass defect ($\Delta m$) for the reaction is:

$\Delta m = m(^{7}_{3}\text{Li}) + m(^{1}_{1}\text{H}) - 2m(^{4}_{2}\text{He})$

$\Delta m = 7.0183 + 1.008 - 2(4.004)$

$\Delta m = 8.0263 - 8.008 = 0.0183 \text{ u}$

Energy released per reaction ($Q$) is:

$Q = \Delta m \times 931 \text{ MeV} = 0.0183 \times 931 \text{ MeV} = 17.0373 \text{ MeV}$

Number of moles of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$ in $\dfrac{7}{17.13} \text{ kg}$:

$n = \dfrac{\dfrac{7}{17.13} \times 10^3 \text{ g}}{7 \text{ g/mol}} = \dfrac{1000}{17.13} \text{ mol}$

Number of atoms of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$:

$N = n \times N_A = \dfrac{1000}{17.13} \times 6.0 \times 10^{23} = \dfrac{6}{17.13} \times 10^{26}$

Total energy released ($E$) is:

$E = N \times Q = \left(\dfrac{6}{17.13} \times 10^{26}\right) \times (17.0373 \times 10^6 \text{ eV})$

$E = \dfrac{102.2238}{17.13} \times 10^{32} \text{ eV} \approx 5.967 \times 10^{32} \text{ eV}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $\alpha = 6$.

Answer: $6$

The half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is same as the average life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are decay constants of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> respectively, then choose the correct relation from the given options.
question: Two radioactive elements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> initially have same number of atoms. The half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is same as the average life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are decay constants of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> respectively, then choose the correct relation from the given options.
solution:
It is given that 

T12(A)=Tav(B)

The half life of a substance is given by T12=ln2λ

So,

ln2λA=1λB

⇒λA=λBln2

subject: Physics
question:
The binding energy per nucleon of $^{209}_{83}Bi$ is _______ MeV.
$[\text{Take } m(^{209}_{83}Bi) = 208.980388 \, u, \, m_p = 1.007825 \, u, \, m_n = 1.008665 \, u, \, 1 \, u = 931 \, \text{MeV}/c^2]$
solution:
For the nucleus $^{209}_{83}Bi$, the number of protons is $Z = 83$ and the number of neutrons is $N = 209 - 83 = 126$.

Mass of $83$ protons $= 83 \times 1.007825 = 83.649475 \, u$

Mass of $126$ neutrons $= 126 \times 1.008665 = 127.091790 \, u$

Total mass of the nucleons $= 83.649475 + 127.091790 = 210.741265 \, u$

The mass defect $\Delta m$ is given by:

$\Delta m = \text{Total mass of nucleons} - \text{Mass of nucleus}$

$\Delta m = 210.741265 - 208.980388 = 1.760877 \, u$

The total binding energy ($BE$) is:

$BE = \Delta m \times 931 \, \text{MeV}$

$BE = 1.760877 \times 931 = 1639.376 \, \text{MeV}$

The binding energy per nucleon is:

$\dfrac{BE}{A} = \dfrac{1639.376}{209} \approx 7.84 \, \text{MeV}$

Answer: $7.84$

subject: Physics
q: The half life of a radioactive substance is 20 minutes. The approximate time interval $\left(t_2-t_1\right)$ between the time $t_2$ when $\frac{2}{3}$ of it has decayed and time $t_1$ and $\frac{1}{3}$ of it had decayed is :
question: The half life of a radioactive substance is 20 minutes. The approximate time interval $\left(t_2-t_1\right)$ between the time $t_2$ when $\frac{2}{3}$ of it has decayed and time $t_1$ and $\frac{1}{3}$ of it had decayed is :
solution:
$\mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{2}}=20$ minutes
$\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}_2} \quad \lambda \mathrm{t}_1=\ln 3$
$\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{~N}_0=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}_2} \mathrm{t}_1=\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln 3$
$\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{~N}_0=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda t_2}$
$\mathrm{t}_2=\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln \frac{3}{2}$
$\mathrm{t}_2-\mathrm{t}_1=\frac{1}{\lambda}\left[\ln \frac{3}{2}-\ln 3\right]$
$=\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln \left[\frac{1}{2}\right]=\frac{0.693}{\lambda}$
$=20 \min$

q: The activity of a radioactive sample falls from <math><mn>700</mn><mi></mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> to <math><mn>500</mn><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in <math><mn>30</mn></math> minutes. Its half life is close to:
question: The activity of a radioactive sample falls from <math><mn>700</mn><mi></mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> to <math><mn>500</mn><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in <math><mn>30</mn></math> minutes. Its half life is close to:
solution:
From equation of radioactivity. At=A0e-λt

         lnA0At=λt

⇒  ln700500=λ(30min) ------1

also

⇒  ln2=λt1/2 --------2

(ii)/(i)

⇒  ln2ln(7/5)=t1/2(30min)  ⇒  (2.06004)30=t1/2=61.8min

≈      62min

subject: Physics
q: A radioactive nucleus decays by two different processes. The half-life for the first process is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and that for the second is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The effective half-life of the nucleus is close to:
question: A radioactive nucleus decays by two different processes. The half-life for the first process is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and that for the second is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The effective half-life of the nucleus is close to:
solution:
Using the equation of radioactive decay law,

-dNdt=λ1N+λ2N  ...(1)

-dNdt=λ1+λ2N  ...(2)

Where,

-dNdt is the rate of decay

λ1, λ2 are decay constant for two different processes

N is the number of atoms.

Now applying the decay law for the combine process, we get

-dNdt=λeffN  ...(3)

Equating equation (1) and (2) we get

λeff=λ1+λ2  ...(4)

Now the half life of the radioactive nucleus is given by,

λ=ln2T  ...(5)

Where, T is the half life of the radioactive nucleus

Using equation (5) equation (4) becomes,

ln2T=ln2T1+ln2T2

1T=1T1+1T2

1T=110+1100=11100

⇒T=10011≈9 s

subject: Physics
q: The radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> of a nucleus of mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> can be estimated by the formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. It follows that the mass density of n nucleus is of the order of: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>M</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></msub><mo>≅</mo><msub><mi>M</mi><mtext>neut </mtext></msub><mo>≃</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>67</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: The radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> of a nucleus of mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> can be estimated by the formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. It follows that the mass density of n nucleus is of the order of: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>M</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></msub><mo>≅</mo><msub><mi>M</mi><mtext>neut </mtext></msub><mo>≃</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>67</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
$\rho_{\text {nucleus }}=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}$
$=\frac{A}{(4 / 3) \pi r_0^3 \mathrm{~A}}=\frac{3}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}_0^3}$
$=2.3 \times 10^{17} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$

question: Starting with a sample of pure ${ }^{66} \mathrm{Cu}, 7 / 8$ of it decays into $\mathrm{Zn}$ in 15 minutes. The corresponding half-life is
solution:
$\mathrm{N}_0 \stackrel{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}{\longrightarrow} \frac{\mathrm{N}_0}{2} \stackrel{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}{\longrightarrow} \frac{\mathrm{N}_0}{4} \stackrel{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}{\longrightarrow} \frac{\mathrm{N}_0}{8}$
$3 \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=15 \therefore \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=5$

question:

[mass of He nucleus = $4.002 \mathrm{amu}, 1 \mathrm{amu}=1.66 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}, \mathrm{~h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}$ and $\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ ]
solution:
$h v =(4 \times 4.002-15.348) \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27} \times\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2$
$v =14.94 \times 10^{19} \mathrm{kHz}$

q: A radio active material is reduced to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> of its original amount in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> days. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of the material is left after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> days the initial amount of the material is
question: A radio active material is reduced to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> of its original amount in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> days. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of the material is left after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> days the initial amount of the material is
solution:
The initial and final amount of the radioactive substance are related by the formula

NN0=12n   ...1

where, n is the number of half lives.

Substitute 18 for NN0 into equation (1) and solve to calculate the value of n.

18=12n⇒123=12n⇒n=3

The half-life of the radioactive element can be calculated as follows:

tt12=n   ...2

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (2) and solve to calculate the half-life.

3 dayst12= 3⇒t12= 1 day

The initial and the final mass of the radioactive element are related by

mm0=12t't12   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) and solve to calculate the initial mass of the substance.

8×10-3 kgm0= 1251⇒m0= 25×8×10-3 kg= 256 g

subject: Physics
q:
Following statements related to radioactivity are given below :
(A) Radioactivity is a random and spontaneous process and is dependent on physical and chemical conditions.
(B) The number of undecayed nuclei in the radioactive sample decays exponentially with time.
(C) Slope of the graph of loge (no. of undecayed nuclei) vs. time represents the reciprocal of mean life time τ.
(D) Product of decay constant λ and half-life time T12 is not constant.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Following statements related to radioactivity are given below :
(A) Radioactivity is a random and spontaneous process and is dependent on physical and chemical conditions.
(B) The number of undecayed nuclei in the radioactive sample decays exponentially with time.
(C) Slope of the graph of loge (no. of undecayed nuclei) vs. time represents the reciprocal of mean life time τ.
(D) Product of decay constant λ and half-life time T12 is not constant.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
Radioactive decay is a random and spontaneous process, it depends on unbalancing of nucleus. This process is dependent on the law of conservation of charge.

We know that, N=N0e-λt    ...1, Where, N=number of decayed nuclei in the sample at time t and N0=initial number of nuclei.

Hence, total number of undecayed nuclei N0-N

Thus, N0-N=N01-e-λt

This shows that the total number of undecayed nuclei decays exponentially with time. 

Taking log both sides of equation 1

lnN=-λt+lnN0

So, slope =-λ  (reciprocal of mean life time)

Half life time T12=ln2λ

Then, λ×T12=ln2=constant

Hence, (B) and (C) are correct only.

subject: Physics
q: When a $U^{238}$ nucleus originally at rest, decays by emitting an alpha particle having a speed ' $u$ ', the recoil speed of the residual nucleus is
question: When a $U^{238}$ nucleus originally at rest, decays by emitting an alpha particle having a speed ' $u$ ', the recoil speed of the residual nucleus is
solution:
Applying the principle of conservation of linear momentum
(4) $(\mathrm{u})=(\mathrm{v})(238) \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{4 \mathrm{u}}{238}$
